name: Market risk — VaR, ES & Greeks
description: 24 interview-screen MCQs — market risk — var, es & greeks. Each card explains the answer.
primerTitle: Market risk — VaR, ES & the Greeks
primer:
## Market risk
**VaR** is a quantile of the loss distribution ('how bad on a normal day?') — it ignores tail severity and can violate **subadditivity**; **Expected Shortfall** (mean loss beyond VaR) is coherent, which is why Basel moved 99% VaR → 97.5% ES. Compute VaR by historical simulation, parametric (z·σ·value), or Monte Carlo; scale by √time for i.i.d. returns; validate by **backtesting** exceptions (Kupiec). Option **Greeks**: delta (∂V/∂S), gamma (∂delta/∂S, convexity), vega (vol), theta (time decay); bonds: modified duration (1st order) and convexity (2nd order).

cards.term: Value at Risk (VaR) at the 99% confidence level over one day is:
choices: The standard deviation of the one-day return distribution
The expected (mean) loss averaged over all days
The expected one-day trading profit on the book
A loss threshold not expected to be exceeded on 99% of days
The largest loss the portfolio could ever conceivably suffer
cards.definition: VaR at 99% over one day is a loss threshold not expected to be exceeded on 99% of days; equivalently, the 99th-percentile quantile of the one-day loss distribution. It answers 'how bad on a normal day?', not 'how bad could it ever get'. The largest loss that could ever occur is wrong because VaR explicitly tolerates a 1% tail of worse outcomes and says nothing about how large those breaches are. It is not the standard deviation of returns (a dispersion measure, though parametric VaR is built from it as z times sigma), nor the expected loss or expected profit, which are central-tendency measures rather than a tail quantile.

cards.term: A key criticism of VaR is that it:
choices: Is a coherent risk measure under every loss distribution
Systematically overstates the portfolio's true level of risk
Is far too easy to backtest reliably to be of any use
Cannot in practice be computed for multi-asset portfolios
Says nothing about the size of losses beyond the threshold
cards.definition: VaR's key weakness is that it pins down a quantile but says nothing about the size of losses once that quantile is breached; two portfolios with identical VaR can have wildly different tail magnitudes. This is exactly what Expected Shortfall (mean loss beyond VaR) fixes. Claiming it is coherent under every loss distribution is false: VaR can violate subadditivity, so it is not coherent in general. It is not too easy to backtest to be useful (counting exceptions is straightforward, which is actually a practical advantage over ES), it does not systematically overstate the true risk (it can understate fat tails), and it can be computed for multi-asset portfolios.

cards.term: Expected Shortfall (ES), also called CVaR, is:
choices: The probability of incurring a loss over the horizon
The average loss given that the loss exceeds the VaR level
Always numerically identical to the VaR at the same level
The median (50th-percentile) of the loss distribution
The single worst loss observed anywhere in the sample
cards.definition: Expected Shortfall, or CVaR, is the average loss conditional on the loss exceeding the VaR level; it integrates the tail rather than reading a single point off it. This captures tail magnitude, which VaR ignores. It is not the probability of incurring any loss (that is a frequency, not a loss size) and not numerically identical to the VaR level (VaR is the threshold, ES is the mean beyond it, so ES is at least as large as VaR). It is not the median or the single worst loss: the median is a central quantile and the worst loss is one extreme point, whereas ES averages over the whole tail beyond VaR.

cards.term: Unlike VaR, Expected Shortfall is a coherent risk measure because it:
choices: Is evaluated at a lower confidence level
Always exceeds the corresponding VaR figure
Requires returns to be normally distributed
Disregards the shape of the loss tail
Satisfies subadditivity and the other coherence axioms
cards.definition: ES is coherent because it satisfies subadditivity along with the other coherence axioms, so a diversified portfolio's ES never exceeds the sum of its parts' ES. VaR can violate subadditivity, making diversification spuriously appear to add risk. Being evaluated at a lower confidence level does not confer coherence; always exceeding the corresponding VaR figure is a consequence, not the defining reason. ES does not require returns to be normally distributed (it is defined for any loss distribution), and it does not disregard the shape of the loss tail; on the contrary it averages over the tail, which is precisely the property that makes it coherent.

cards.term: The four properties defining a coherent risk measure are monotonicity, translation invariance, positive homogeneity, and:
choices: Stationarity
Independence
Normality
Subadditivity
Linearity
cards.definition: The four coherence axioms (Artzner et al.) are monotonicity, translation invariance, positive homogeneity, and subadditivity; subadditivity is the one VaR can fail and ES satisfies. Stationarity and independence are properties of a return process, not of a risk measure, so they are out of scope. Normality is a distributional assumption, not a coherence axiom. Linearity is too strong and is not required: positive homogeneity plus subadditivity gives convexity, not full linearity, since merging portfolios may reduce risk.

cards.term: Subadditivity of a risk measure means:
choices: The risk of a combined portfolio never exceeds the sum of the parts' risks
The risk of any portfolio is always exactly zero
The measure depends only on individual asset correlations
The risk of a combined portfolio always equals the sum of the parts
Combining positions always increases total risk
cards.definition: Subadditivity means the risk of a combined portfolio never exceeds the sum of the parts' risks, formalising the idea that diversification should never increase total risk. The opposite, that combining positions always increases total risk, is the violation a non-coherent measure like VaR can exhibit. The claim that combined risk always equals the sum of the parts is wrong because combination should weakly reduce, not exactly preserve, risk. Depending only on correlations misstates the inequality, and risk always being exactly zero is simply false.

cards.term: Historical-simulation VaR is computed by:
choices: Taking the simple sample average of all past returns
Assuming returns follow a normal distribution and scaling sigma
Applying the empirical distribution of past returns to today's positions
Drawing returns at random from a fitted statistical model
Pricing every position with the Black-Scholes option formula
cards.definition: Historical-simulation VaR applies the empirical distribution of past returns to today's positions: revalue current holdings under each historical move and read the quantile off the resulting loss distribution. It is non-parametric, making no distributional assumption, which is its main appeal and contrast to other methods. Assuming a normal distribution describes parametric VaR, and drawing returns from a fitted statistical model describes Monte Carlo VaR. Taking the sample average of past returns gives a central estimate, not a tail quantile, and pricing with Black-Scholes values options rather than estimating portfolio VaR.

cards.term: Parametric (variance-covariance) VaR typically assumes:
choices: No distributional form for the returns is assumed at all
Returns are strongly non-stationary across the whole sample
Returns are drawn from a flat, bounded uniform distribution
Portfolio losses are capped below a fixed known maximum
Returns are normal, so VaR is a multiple of the standard deviation
cards.definition: Parametric (variance-covariance) VaR assumes returns are normal, so VaR collapses to a multiple of the portfolio standard deviation: VaR = z times sigma times value. Its speed comes from this closed form, but it can understate risk when returns are fat-tailed. Assuming no distributional form at all describes historical simulation, not the parametric method. A uniform distribution is not the assumption used, non-stationary returns would break the single-sigma estimate, and bounded losses are not assumed: the normal tail is unbounded.

cards.term: Monte Carlo VaR works by:
choices: Replaying only the actually observed historical return paths
Setting every cross-asset correlation in the book to exactly zero
Simply counting the past threshold exceptions over the window
Simulating many scenarios from an assumed model and reading the quantile
Solving one closed-form normal formula directly for the quantile
cards.definition: Monte Carlo VaR simulates many return scenarios from an assumed model and reads the loss quantile off the simulated distribution. Its strength is flexibility: it handles nonlinear payoffs and complex instruments that closed forms cannot, at the cost of being computationally heavy and model-dependent. Replaying only observed historical returns describes historical simulation, and solving a closed-form normal formula describes parametric VaR. It does not set correlations to zero (they are built into the simulating model) and it does not merely count past exceptions, which is backtesting.

cards.term: Under the assumption of i.i.d. returns, 10-day VaR is obtained from 1-day VaR by multiplying by:
choices: The square root of 252
The square root of 10
cards.definition: For i.i.d. returns, volatility scales with the square root of time, so 10-day VaR equals 1-day VaR times the square root of 10. Variance grows linearly in the horizon, hence standard deviation grows as the square root. Multiplying by 10 would wrongly assume risk scales with variance rather than its square root; multiplying by 1 ignores horizon entirely; the square root of 252 is the annualisation factor for daily-to-yearly, not 10-day; and 100 confuses the variance scaling (10 squared) with the volatility scaling.

cards.term: Backtesting a VaR model involves:
choices: Counting how often actual losses exceeded the VaR versus the expected rate
Re-estimating the portfolio's mean return from recent data
Running a battery of extreme scenarios and nothing else
Testing the return series for the presence of a unit root
Computing the portfolio's realised Sharpe ratio over the window
cards.definition: Backtesting counts how often actual losses exceeded the VaR and compares that exception rate to the expected rate (1% of days for a 99% VaR). A large discrepancy in either direction flags a miscalibrated model, formalised by tests like Kupiec's. Re-estimating the mean return and testing for a unit root are time-series estimation tasks, not validation of tail coverage. Running extreme scenarios and nothing else is a different, scenario-based exercise, and the Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return, not VaR accuracy.

cards.term: Stress testing complements VaR by:
choices: Deliberately leaving severe tail events out of the analysis
Evaluating losses under specific extreme but plausible scenarios
Reporting only the portfolio's average daily level of risk
Removing the need to compute statistical VaR at all
Assuming markets always behave under normal-distribution conditions
cards.definition: Stress testing evaluates losses under specific extreme but plausible scenarios, probing the rare, severe, or unprecedented moves that statistical VaR can miss. It complements rather than replaces VaR, so removing the need to compute VaR is wrong. Leaving tail events out is the opposite of its purpose, reporting average daily risk is what routine VaR does, and assuming markets always behave normally contradicts the very premise of stressing for abnormal conditions.

cards.term: The option Greek 'delta' measures the sensitivity of the option price to:
choices: A change in the underlying's volatility
A change in the risk-free interest rate
A change in the underlying asset's price
A change in the contract's strike
The passage of time to expiry
cards.definition: Delta measures the sensitivity of the option price to a change in the underlying asset's price; it is the first derivative of value with respect to spot and the core quantity in delta-hedging. Sensitivity to volatility is vega, sensitivity to the risk-free rate is rho, and the passage of time is theta. Sensitivity to the strike is not a standard traded Greek, since the strike is fixed by contract; delta is specifically about moves in the underlying.

cards.term: 'Gamma' measures:
choices: The rate of change of delta as the underlying moves
The decay of value as time passes
The sensitivity of value to volatility
The option's intrinsic value level
The sensitivity of value to interest rates
cards.definition: Gamma is the rate of change of delta as the underlying moves, the option's convexity and the second derivative of value with respect to spot. High gamma means delta shifts rapidly, forcing frequent rebalancing of a delta hedge, and gamma peaks near the money. The decay of value as time passes is theta, sensitivity to volatility is vega, and sensitivity to interest rates is rho. Intrinsic value is a price level, not a sensitivity, so it is not a Greek.

cards.term: 'Vega' measures an option's sensitivity to:
choices: A change in the risk-free rate
A change in the volatility of the underlying
A change in the underlying's price
A change in the expected dividend
The passage of time to expiry
cards.definition: Vega measures an option's sensitivity to a change in the volatility of the underlying; long option positions are long vega and gain when implied volatility rises. Sensitivity to the underlying's price is delta, the passage of time is theta, and sensitivity to the risk-free rate is rho. Sensitivity to the expected dividend (sometimes called epsilon) is a separate, minor Greek and is not vega, which is specifically the volatility sensitivity.

cards.term: 'Theta' measures:
choices: The sensitivity of value to the underlying
The change in option value as time passes
The sensitivity of value to interest rates
The rate of change of the option's delta
The sensitivity of value to volatility
cards.definition: Theta measures the change in option value as time passes, i.e. time decay; for long options it is typically negative because, all else equal, options lose value as expiry approaches. Sensitivity to the underlying is delta, sensitivity to volatility is vega, and sensitivity to interest rates is rho. The rate of change of delta is gamma, a different second-order Greek, not the effect of time.

cards.term: Modified duration of a bond approximates:
choices: The bond's risk-neutral probability of defaulting
The bond's quoted yield to maturity at issue
The percentage price change for a small change in yield
The bond's stated annual coupon rate of interest
The bond's remaining maturity in years, exactly
cards.definition: Modified duration approximates the percentage price change for a small change in yield: price change is roughly minus modified duration times the yield change. It is a first-order (linear) interest-rate sensitivity. It is not the yield to maturity or the coupon rate, which are contractual rate quantities, and not the probability of default, which is a credit measure. It is not the bond's maturity in years exactly: Macaulay duration is a time measure, and modified duration adjusts it by dividing by (1 plus yield), so they coincide only as an approximation.

cards.term: Convexity of a bond captures:
choices: The curvature of the price-yield relationship
The expected recovery rate on default
The bond's credit spread over Treasuries
The schedule of coupon payments
The first-order price sensitivity to yield
cards.definition: Convexity captures the curvature of the price-yield relationship, the second-order effect that refines the linear duration estimate. Positive convexity benefits the holder: prices rise more when yields fall than they fall when yields rise by the same amount. The first-order price sensitivity to yield is duration, not convexity. The recovery rate, credit spread, and coupon schedule are credit or cash-flow attributes, not the second-order shape of the price-yield curve.

cards.term: A bond with modified duration 5 will, for a 100 basis point (1%) rise in yield, change in price by approximately:
cards.definition: Using price change is approximately minus duration times yield change, the answer is -5 times 0.01 = -5%; rising yields lower bond prices, so the move is negative. +5% has the wrong sign. -1% and -0.5% ignore the duration multiplier and effectively use a duration near 1 or smaller. -10% doubles the correct figure, as if duration were 10 rather than 5. Convexity would add a small positive correction, slightly softening the -5%.

cards.term: A 1-day 99% parametric VaR for a 1,000,000 EUR portfolio with daily volatility 2% (using z = 2.33, zero mean) is approximately:
cards.definition: Parametric VaR = z times sigma times value = 2.33 times 0.02 times 1,000,000 = 46,600 EUR. The 20,000 figure drops the z-multiplier (just sigma times value). The 23,300 figure uses 2.33 times 0.01, halving the volatility. The 2,000 figure is off by an order of magnitude, and 100,000 corresponds to a 10% move, far above the 2.33-sigma daily loss implied here.

cards.term: Marginal (or incremental) VaR measures:
choices: The change in portfolio VaR from a small change in a position
The standalone VaR of a single asset valued in isolation
The expected (mean) loss of the portfolio over the horizon
The total aggregate VaR of the entire portfolio at once
The expected shortfall of the portfolio beyond its VaR
cards.definition: Marginal (incremental) VaR measures the change in total portfolio VaR from a small change in a given position, capturing that position's contribution to overall risk including diversification effects. This guides hedging and limit-setting by flagging the biggest risk contributors. The VaR of a single asset valued in isolation ignores those portfolio interactions. The total aggregate VaR is the whole, not the marginal contribution; the expected loss and the expected shortfall are entirely different measures.

cards.term: Liquidity risk refers to the risk that:
choices: An asset cannot be traded quickly without a significant price concession
Return volatility clusters into alternating calm and turbulent periods
The pricing model used to value the book is itself misspecified
A trading counterparty fails to honour its contractual obligations
Prevailing market interest rates move sharply and against your position
cards.definition: Liquidity risk is the risk that an asset cannot be traded quickly without a significant price concession, i.e. you cannot exit at fair value. A counterparty failing to pay is credit risk, interest rates moving is market risk, and a misspecified pricing model is model risk. Volatility clustering is a statistical property of returns, not a risk category. Note this asset/market-liquidity dimension is distinct from funding liquidity, the risk that financing itself dries up.

cards.term: The EWMA and GARCH approaches improve on a simple equally weighted volatility estimate by:
choices: Stripping all of the autocorrelation out of the return series
Discarding the most recent observations as too noisy
Weighting recent observations more, capturing changing volatility
Holding the estimated volatility constant through all regimes
Relying only on low-frequency annual data points
cards.definition: EWMA and GARCH weight recent observations more, letting the volatility estimate track changing market conditions instead of treating all sample days equally. Recent returns are more informative about today's volatility, so weighting them up improves risk estimates and captures volatility clustering. Holding volatility constant is exactly the equally weighted estimate they improve upon. Discarding recent observations and relying only on annual data throw away the most relevant information, and stripping all autocorrelation out of returns is not what these volatility models do.

cards.term: If a 99% one-day VaR is exceeded on about 5% of days in backtesting, the model is most likely:
choices: Actually measuring expected shortfall instead
Suffering from regression multicollinearity
Overstating the true level of risk
Essentially perfectly calibrated
Understating risk, with too many exceptions
cards.definition: A correctly calibrated 99% one-day VaR should be breached on roughly 1% of days; seeing about 5% exceptions means losses pierce the threshold far too often, so the model is understating risk with too many exceptions. Overstating the true level of risk would show too few exceptions, not too many. Essentially perfectly calibrated would be about 1%, not 5%. The exception count has nothing to do with measuring expected shortfall, and multicollinearity is a regression diagnostic, not a VaR backtesting outcome.
